original_abstract:
This paper introduces Llama 3, a new family of foundation models designed to support multilinguality, coding, reasoning, and tool usage across varying scales. The flagship model is a dense Transformer featuring 405 billion parameters and a 128K-token context window, trained on an extensive corpus of over 15 trillion tokens. Through rigorous empirical evaluation, the authors demonstrate that Llama 3.1 405B achieves performance comparable to leading closed-source models, such as GPT-4, across a diverse array of benchmarks.

The development process emphasizes three primary levers: high-quality data curation, massive computational scale, and the management of architectural complexity. The authors utilize an iterative post-training pipeline—incorporating rejection sampling, supervised finetuning, and direct preference optimization—to align the models with human preferences and enhance specific technical capabilities. In addition to the text-based models, the paper explores a compositional approach to multimodality, integrating image, video, and speech recognizers into the Llama 3 framework. While the multimodal variants remain under active development, the 8B, 70B, and 405B language models, along with enhanced safety classifiers like Llama Guard 3, are publicly released to foster open innovation and the responsible development of artificial general intelligence.
humanized_abstract:
We introduce Llama 3, a new family of foundation models supporting multilinguality, coding, reasoning and tool use on all scales. The core model is a dense Transformer with 405B parameters and a 128K-token context window, trained on a dataset of 15 trillion tokens. We validate the performance of our Llama 3.1 405B model through extensive experimentation, showing that its performance on our suite of benchmarks is on par with current closed-source models like GPT-4.

The main focus of our paper is on three of these levers and describes our data curation, scale computing and software architecture strategies. To match our models to human preferences and improve specific skills, we used an iterative post-training regimen of rejection sampling, supervised fine-tuning and direct preference optimization. We also describe a compositional approach to multimodality that seamlessly integrates image, video and speech recognition into the Llama 3 infrastructure. Multimodal variants of the models are currently under development, but the 8B, 70B and 405B models as well as the new safety classifiers — Llama Guard 3 — will be publicly available in order to encourage an open innovation process and help ensure that the AGI is built in a responsible manner.

The main focus of our paper is on three of these levers and describes our data curation, scale computing and software architecture strategies. To match our models to human preferences and improve specific skills, we used an iterative post-training regimen of rejection sampling, supervised fine-tuning and direct preference optimization. We also describe a compositional approach to multimodality that seamlessly integrates image, video and speech recognition into the Llama 3 infrastructure. Multimodal variants of the models are currently under development, but the 8B, 70B and 405B models as well as the new safety classifiers — Llama Guard 3 — will be publicly available in order to encourage an open innovation process and help ensure that the AGI is built in a responsible manner.
